other_observers: Ethan Cleveland was with me when I found the bird.  Several other observers showed up after we got the word out.
duration: Thirty minutes or so over a two hour period
description:
Juvenile light/intermediate morph jaeger, first spotted by me on the water.  Took several flights, pursuing Ring-billed Gulls aggressively, and allowing for good views and photographs by Ethan.
Field identification was largely based on GISS impressions -- the bird's head shape seemed gull-like, with a stout sharply bicolored bill.  The bird's wings were broad with signficiant underwing flash.  In direct comparison to Ring-billed Gulls it pursued, it seemed to be slightly larger than the gull.  On the water, the undertail coverts appeared strongly barred and white-based, not buffy.  Its behavior was aggressive and its flight was powerful.  When the bird tired of harassing gulls, it would ride thermals and soar quite high, behavior I have never seen of a jaeger on a pelagic or seawatch but I did see with the Pomarine at Chatfield in October.I reviewed photographs taken by Ethan Cleveland, Cole Sage, and Isaac Boardman to solidify the ID.  The photographs provide a similar GISS impression of a broad-winged, bulky bird with a gull-shaped head, a thick neck, and a stout bill.  In addition, the photographs show the following plumage features, which together suggest Pomarine Jaeger to me:- White bases to the underwing primary coverts, forming a "double flash"- Neatly barred undertail coverts, appearing white especially in Cole's photos, and uppertail coverts strongly barred as well- 5ish white primary shafts- Underwing coverts, especially the axilliaries, are neatly barred, not wavy- Central tail feathers seem short and blunt, especially in Isaac's photos- Head generally smooth brown in color; although it has a paler nape "Golden Eagle" effect especially in my poor digiscopes, it doesn't have a pale collar or a dark-capped appearance- Overall coloration is gray-brownish, without rusty tones.  Isaac's photos taken in late afternoon light seem buffier because of the lighting but not cinnamon or rusty- I believe I see eye arcs in Isaac's best photo, which is generally seen in Pom/Long-tailed but not Parasitic. The only photos I took were poor digiscopes and are not helpful for ID, so I won't upload them here.  Instead I will link to eBird checklists by Ethan Cleveland, Cole Sage, and Isaac Boardman, where good-quality photos of the bird in flight are available:https://ebird.org/checklist/S283153354https://ebird.org/checklist/S283183838https://ebird.org/checklist/S283205757
